sections.title: What conditionals do (core logic)
sections.content:
Conditionals link two clauses — **if X, then Y** — and the **pair of tenses** on either side of *if* tells the listener how **real** or **unreal** the situation is.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Conditionals sit at the heart of the Clause Linking root (#4 in the Tree Model) — they are the most powerful clause-linker English has. They also pull in the Verb Phrase root, because each conditional type uses a specific tense combination. Master the four tense-pairings and you can speculate, hypothesise, regret, warn, promise, and negotiate — all the high-stakes moves in conversation and writing.

**Two simple rules** that solve most errors:
- **No *will* in an if-clause for the first conditional.** *If it rains* — not *if it will rain*.
- **No *would* in an if-clause for the second / third.** *If I had time* — not *if I would have time*.

**Czech-speaker note**: *kdyby* covers both second and third conditional in Czech. In English you must pick: past tense for present/future hypotheticals (*if I had…*) vs past perfect for past hypotheticals (*if I had had…*). Choosing the wrong one is the #1 conditional error.

sections.title: The four types at a glance
sections.content:
A single table is worth a hundred bullet points here. Learn the **four tense-pairings** and the **use** that goes with each — everything else is detail.

**Why it matters**: When you hear or write *If + (tense)…*, the tense alone signals the speaker's stance toward reality. Wrong tense pair = wrong meaning, not just wrong grammar.

diagram.title: The four conditional types
rows: If it **rains**, I'**ll stay** at home.
If I **had** time, I **would help** you.
If I **had known**, I **would have told** you.

sections.title: Real conditionals — Zero & First
sections.content:
The **real** conditionals describe situations the speaker treats as **actually possible** or **factually true**.

**Why it matters**: These two cover everyday talk about cause-and-effect, plans, warnings, and instructions. They are also the foundation — get these solid before moving to the unreal types.

**Contrast — Zero vs First**:
- **Zero** (general truth, always-true cause-and-effect): *If you **heat** water to 100°, it **boils**.* / *If you **mix** red and blue, you **get** purple.* — Both clauses in **present**.
- **First** (specific real future possibility): *If it **rains** tomorrow, I'**ll stay** home.* / *If you **finish** early, **call** me.* — Present in the if-clause, **will + base** (or imperative) in the main clause.

**Czech-speaker trap**: Don't put *will* in the if-clause. **Wrong**: *If it will rain…* **Right**: *If it **rains**, I will stay.* Czech uses *bude pršet* (future) inside *jestli/když*; English does not.

**Zero or First?** When the result is a general law of nature/behaviour → Zero. When it's a one-off future event → First. (*If I see him, I'll tell him* = First; *If you press this button, the light comes on* = Zero.)

sections.title: Unreal present — Second conditional
sections.content:
**If + past simple, would + base.** The past tense doesn't mean past time here — it signals **distance from reality**. The situation is **imaginary** or **contrary to fact** right now.

**Why it matters**: This is the workhorse for speculation, advice, daydreams, and polite suggestion — *If I were you…*, *If I had a million crowns…*, *If we lived in Italy…* Exam tasks (sentence transformation, CAE/FCE Use of English) lean on it heavily.

**Contrast**:
- *If I **had** more time, I **would help** you.* — I don't have time (now); helping is hypothetical.
- *If we **lived** in Prague, we **would see** each other more.* — We don't live there.

**Formal *were* for all persons**: *If I **were** you, I **would apologise**.* / *If he **were** here, he **would explain**.* In informal speech you'll hear *was*, but exams and writing expect *were*.

**Czech-speaker traps**:
- **Don't translate *kdybych měl* literally.** **Wrong**: *If I would have time…* **Right**: *If I **had** time, I would help.* Past simple in the if-clause, never *would*.
- The English past tense after *if* is **not** a real past — it's the unreal-marker. *If I knew* = *Kdybych věděl*, not *Když jsem věděl*.

sections.title: Unreal past — Third conditional
sections.content:
**If + past perfect, would have + past participle.** For situations in the **past that did not happen** — regrets, counterfactuals, alternative histories.

**Why it matters**: Every time you say *if only*, *I should have*, *what if I'd*… you're reaching for the third conditional. It's the grammar of hindsight, blame, and what-might-have-been. High frequency in storytelling, interviews, and exam writing tasks.

**Contrast**:
- *If I **had known**, I **would have told** you.* — But I didn't know, so I didn't tell you.
- *If she **had left** earlier, she **would have caught** the train.* — She didn't leave early; she missed it.
- *If you **hadn't helped** me, I **would have failed**.* — You did help; I didn't fail.

**Czech-speaker traps**:
- **Don't use *would have* in the if-clause.** **Wrong**: *If I would have known…* **Right**: *If I **had known**, I would have come.* (The *would-have* belongs only in the main clause.)
- **Don't mix Third and Second.** **Wrong**: *If I had known, I would tell you.* (Past condition + present result — only works as a Mixed conditional, see next card.) For pure past, both clauses need the past-perfect / would-have structure: *I **would have told** you.*

sections.title: Mixed conditionals
sections.content:
**Mixed conditionals** combine a condition and a result from **different time-frames** — usually a past condition with a present result, or vice versa.

**Why it matters**: Real life isn't always purely past or purely present. The most common mix — *past cause → present consequence* — is essential for talking about how earlier choices shape your current situation. Common in CAE/FCE and in any reflective conversation about life decisions.

**Two main patterns**:
- **Past condition → present result** (past perfect + would + base): *If I **had studied** harder at school, I **would have** a better job now.* / *If she **hadn't moved** to Prague, we **wouldn't be** together today.*
- **Present condition → past result** (past simple + would have + past participle): *If I **were** more careful, I **wouldn't have lost** my keys yesterday.* / *If he **weren't** so shy, he **would have asked** her out.*

**Tip**: Look for **time markers** (*now*, *today*, *yesterday*, *at school*) — they often tell you you're dealing with a mix, not a pure third or second.

sections.title: Wishes & if only
sections.content:
**Wishes** use the same tense-distancing logic as conditionals — past tense for the present, past perfect for the past — to express **regret**, **longing**, or **complaint**.

**Why it matters**: *I wish* and *if only* are how English handles the emotional layer of conditionals — what you want to be true but isn't, or what you'd undo if you could. High frequency in conversation and writing.

**Three patterns**:
- **Present wish** — *I wish I **had** more time.* / *If only I **knew** the answer.* (Past tense → present hypothetical, same as Second conditional.)
- **Past wish / regret** — *I wish I **had studied** harder.* / *If only she **hadn't said** that.* (Past perfect → past hypothetical, same as Third conditional.)
- **Wish about behaviour (annoyance)** — *I wish you **would stop** interrupting.* / *If only it **would rain**.* (*would* + base — used about someone else's behaviour you can't control.)

**Czech-speaker note**: *Kéž by* covers all three; English splits them by tense. And: you can't say *I wish I would…* about yourself — only about other people or impersonal forces.

sections.title: Variants — unless, as long as, provided that…
sections.content:
*If* isn't the only word that introduces a conditional. Several **alternatives** add shades of meaning — restriction, exception, precondition.

**Why it matters**: These appear constantly in formal writing, contracts, instructions, and CAE-level reading. Knowing them lets you express conditions more precisely than *if* alone.

**The main set**:
- **unless** = *if not* — *I'll be there at 7 **unless** the train is late.* (= if it isn't late)
- **as long as / so long as** = on the condition that — *You can borrow it **as long as** you return it tomorrow.*
- **provided (that) / providing (that)** = only if — formal — *We'll sign **provided that** the terms don't change.*
- **in case** = as a precaution against — *Take an umbrella **in case** it rains.* (Note: **not** the same as *if*. *Take an umbrella if it rains* = only take it then. *In case it rains* = take it now, just in case.)
- **supposing / suppose** = imagine if — *Supposing he doesn't come, what will we do?*
- **otherwise** = if not (linking two sentences) — *Hurry up; **otherwise** we'll be late.*

**Czech-speaker trap**: *unless* is one negative idea, not two. **Wrong**: *unless you don't tell him* (double negative). **Right**: ***unless** you tell him* (= if you don't tell him).

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors — quick reference
sections.content:
The errors below account for the large majority of conditional mistakes from Czech speakers. Fixing these gives immediate visible progress.

**Why it matters**: Most of these come from word-for-word translation from Czech. Once you see the pattern, you can self-correct in real time.

**The top traps**:
- **First — no *will* in the if-clause**: Wrong *If I **will see** him, I'll tell him.* → Right *If I **see** him, I'll tell him.*
- **Second — no *would* in the if-clause**: Wrong *If I **would have** time…* → Right *If I **had** time, I would help.*
- **Third — no *would have* in the if-clause**: Wrong *If I **would have known**…* → Right *If I **had known**, I would have come.*
- **Third — both clauses need the past-perfect / would-have structure**: Wrong *If I had known, I **would tell** you.* → Right *I **would have told** you.*
- **Second — formal *were* not *was***: Wrong *If I **was** you…* → Right (in writing/exams) *If I **were** you…*
- **unless — no double negative**: Wrong *unless you **don't** tell him* → Right ***unless** you tell him*
- ***až* ≠ *until***: Czech *až přijde* often means *when he comes*, not *until he comes*. Check the meaning before translating.

questions.question: Which type of conditional is: 'If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.'?
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present + present for general/scientific facts.

questions.question: Choose the correct first conditional.

options.a: If it will rain, we will stay at home.
options.b: If it rained, we would stay at home.
options.c: If it rains, we will stay at home.
options.d: If it rains, we would stay at home.

questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present, will + base. No 'will' in the if-clause.

questions.question: 'If I _____ you, I would apologise.' (Second conditional)
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past. 'Were' is used for all persons in formal style (subjunctive).

questions.question: Which is a third conditional?

options.a: If I know, I will tell you.
options.b: If I had known, I would tell you.
options.c: If I knew, I would have told you.
options.d: If I had known, I would have told you.

questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle (unreal past).

questions.question: What is wrong? 'If I would have time, I would help you.'

options.a: We must use 'had' in the if-clause, not 'would have'.
options.b: We must use 'will' in the main clause.
options.c: We must use past perfect in both clauses.

questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (if I had time), not would have.

questions.question: 'If she _____ earlier, she would have caught the train.' (Third conditional)
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect (had left).

questions.question: Zero: If you heat ice, it _____.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: if + present, present (it melts).

questions.question: First: If it _____ tomorrow, we will stay at home.
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (rains), will + base.

questions.question: Second: If I _____ more time, I would help you.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (had), would + base.

questions.question: Second: If I were you, I _____ apologise.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: would + base verb (would apologise).

questions.question: Third: If I _____ known, I would have told you.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect (had known).

questions.question: Third: If she had left earlier, she _____ the train.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle.

questions.question: First: If you _____ early, call me.
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (finish), main clause can be imperative.

questions.question: Zero: If you don't sleep well, you _____ tired.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present in both clauses (you feel).

questions.question: Second: If we _____ the lottery, we would travel the world.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (won).

questions.question: Third: If they hadn't missed the flight, they _____ on time.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle (would have arrived).

questions.question: Zero: If you heat ice, it _____.
answers: melts
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present + present for facts.

questions.question: First: If it _____ tomorrow, we will stay at home.
answers: rains
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present (rains), will in main clause.

questions.question: Second: If I _____ (have) more time, I would help you.
answers: had
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple.

questions.question: Second: If I were you, I _____ (apologise).
answers: would apologise
questions.explanation: Second conditional: would + base in main clause.

questions.question: Third: If I _____ (know), I would have told you.
answers: had known
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect.

questions.question: Third: If she had left earlier, she _____ (catch) the train.
answers: would have caught
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle in main clause.

questions.question: First: If you _____ early, call me. (one word; present simple, e.g. finish or leave)
answers: finish
arrive
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple; main clause can be imperative. Common verbs: finish, arrive.

questions.question: Zero: If you don't sleep well, you _____ tired.
answers: feel
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present in both clauses.

questions.question: Second: If we _____ (win) the lottery, we would travel the world.
answers: won
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (won, not would win).

questions.question: Third: If they hadn't missed the flight, they _____ (arrive) on time.
answers: would have arrived
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I will see him, I'll tell him.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I see him, I'll tell him.
If I see him, I'll tell him
questions.explanation: First conditional: no 'will' in the if-clause; use present.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I would have time, I would help.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had time, I would help.
If I had time, I would help
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (had), not would have.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I would have known, I would have come.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had known, I would have come.
If I had known, I would have come
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if-clause uses past perfect (had known), not would have.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I had known, I would tell you.' (Meaning: I didn't know, so I didn't tell.) Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had known, I would have told you.
If I had known, I would have told you
questions.explanation: Third conditional: both clauses refer to the past – would have told.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If it will rain, we will stay at home.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If it rains, we will stay at home.
If it rains, we will stay at home
questions.explanation: First conditional: present in if-clause (if it rains).

questions.question: Wrong: 'If she had left earlier, she would catch the train.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train.
If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train
questions.explanation: Third conditional: main clause needs would have + past participle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If you heat water to 100 degrees, it will boil.' (General fact.) Correct to zero conditional.
answers: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils
questions.explanation: Zero conditional for general truth: present + present (it boils).

questions.question: Wrong: 'If he would try harder, he could pass.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If he tried harder, he could pass.
If he tried harder, he could pass
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (tried), not would try.

mc.title: Practice: Conditionals (zero, first, second, third) (multiple choice)
